title: Boston brown bread
subtitle: Dark, moist steamed cylindrical bread with molasses
excerpt: A cylindrical steamed bread from New England, made with a mix of wholemeal, rye, and plain flours sweetened with molasses. Leavened with bicarbonate of soda and steamed rather than baked, it produces an exceptionally moist, tender crumb with a sweet, earthy flavour. Traditionally cooked in tin cans or pudding basins set in a covered pot of simmering water for about an hour, with no oven required.
sourceNotes: Boston brown bread is documented in New England cookery literature from the mid-nineteenth century, associated with the Saturday supper tradition of serving it alongside baked beans. Fannie Farmer, The Boston Cooking-School Cook Book (first edition 1896, fully in the public domain and available at Project Gutenberg), gives a recipe using cornmeal, rye, and graham flour with molasses, steamed in a covered tin. The version here uses a more accessible flour combination while preserving the molasses, steaming method, and cylindrical shape.

recipe.freezeNotes: Cool completely, slice, and freeze. Defrost at room temperature or warm in a covered oven dish with a splash of water.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps well wrapped at room temperature for 2 days. Traditionally served with baked beans; also good with cream cheese or smoked salmon.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: Steamed on the hob; no oven required. Maintain a gentle simmer for the full hour; rapid boiling rocks the basin and causes uneven cooking.

recipeTools.notes: 1-litre pudding basin, greased and base-lined, or a clean 400 g tin can with both ends removed and one replaced.
Deep enough to hold the basin with a lid on the pan.

glossaryTerms.definition: Sealing the top of a pudding basin or tin used for steamed bread with a double layer of foil or greaseproof paper, pleated across the middle to allow the bread to expand as it rises during steaming. The covering prevents condensed steam dripping back in and making the bread soggy, while still allowing expansion. Secure with kitchen string tied just below the rim. Do not use a tight, unpleated cover: the bread will be compressed and dense.

content.text: Steaming is what gives Boston brown bread its unique texture: the closed, moist environment produces a loaf with no crust and a uniformly tender, almost pudding-like interior. The basin must be
covered with a pleated foil lid
before going into the pot, and the water in the pan must simmer steadily for the full hour without boiling dry. Check the water level halfway through.

Grease a 1-litre pudding basin generously and line the base with a circle of baking paper. Stir together the three flours, bicarbonate of soda, and salt in a large bowl. Whisk the molasses into the buttermilk, then pour into the flour mixture and stir until combined. Fold in the raisins if using. Pour the batter into the prepared basin; it should come no more than two-thirds up the sides.

Cover the basin with a double layer of foil with a pleat across the centre, and secure with kitchen string. Set the basin on a trivet or upturned saucer in a large saucepan. Pour in enough boiling water to come halfway up the sides of the basin. Cover the pan with a tight-fitting lid and simmer gently for 55 to 65 minutes until the bread is firm to the touch and a skewer inserted through the foil comes out clean. Top up the water if it runs low. Remove and cool for 15 minutes before unmoulding onto a rack.
